coredata.dc:title: Impact of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots on photosynthesis efficiency of rice and corn crops

coredata.dc:description: Photosynthesis is one of the most fundamental biochemical processes on earth such that it is vital to the existence of most lives on this planet. In fact, unravelling the potentials in enhancing photosynthetic efficiency and electron transfer process, which are thought to improve plant growth is one of the emerging approaches in tackling modern agricultural shortcomings. In light of this, zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQD) have emerged and garnered much interest in recent years which can enhance photosynthesis by modulating the associated electron transfer process. In this work, CQD was extracted from empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar using a green acid-free microwave method. The resulting CQD was characterized using HRTEM, PL, UV–Vis and XPS. Typical rice (C3) and corn (C4) crops were selected in the present study in order to compare the significant effect of CQD on the two different photosynthetic pathways of crops. CQD was first introduced into crop via foliar spraying application instead of localised placement of CQD before seedling development. The influence of CQD on the photosynthetic efficiency of rice (C3) and corn (C4) leaves was determined by measuring both carbon dioxide conversion and the stomatal conductance of the leaf. As a result, the introduction of CQD greatly enhanced the photosynthesis in CQD-exposed crops. This is the first study focusing on phylogenetically constrained differences in photosynthetic responses between C3 and C4 crops upon CQD exposure, which gives a better insight into the understanding of photosynthesis process and shows considerable promise in nanomaterial research for sustainable agriculture practices.

head.ce:title: Impact of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots on photosynthesis efficiency of rice and corn crops

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photosynthesis is one of the most fundamental biochemical processes on earth such that it is vital to the existence of most lives on this planet. In fact, unravelling the potentials in enhancing photosynthetic efficiency and electron transfer process, which are thought to improve plant growth is one of the emerging approaches in tackling modern agricultural shortcomings. In light of this, zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQD) have emerged and garnered much interest in recent years which can enhance photosynthesis by modulating the associated electron transfer process. In this work, CQD was extracted from empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar using a green acid-free microwave method. The resulting CQD was characterized using HRTEM, PL, UV–Vis and XPS. Typical rice (C3) and corn (C4) crops were selected in the present study in order to compare the significant effect of CQD on the two different photosynthetic pathways of crops. CQD was first introduced into crop via foliar spraying application instead of localised placement of CQD before seedling development. The influence of CQD on the photosynthetic efficiency of rice (C3) and corn (C4) leaves was determined by measuring both carbon dioxide conversion and the stomatal conductance of the leaf. As a result, the introduction of CQD greatly enhanced the photosynthesis in CQD-exposed crops. This is the first study focusing on phylogenetically constrained differences in photosynthetic responses between C3 and C4 crops upon CQD exposure, which gives a better insight into the understanding of photosynthesis process and shows considerable promise in nanomaterial research for sustainable agriculture practices.

ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthesis is the basis of plant growth; a foundation for organisms to exist which maintains the carbon and oxygen cycle on Earth (Gitelson et al., 2014; Porcar-Castell et al., 2014). In the natural light process, photo-induced transfer of energy and electrons are utilized by photosynthetic organisms to drive their metabolism. Such process includes the trapping of solar energy by photosynthetic units (PSUs) formed by the association of light-harvesting complexes (LHCs), which plays a pivotal role as a light-harvesting antenna. This is followed by the transfer of electrons to specialist chlorophyll cofactors in specialised reaction centres (RC). This region is where the captured energy is converted into chemical energy in the chloroplasts and stored in the bonds of sugar (6H2O + 6CO2 + solar energy → C6H12O6 + 6O2) (Maurino and Weber, 2013; Maxwell and Johnson, 2000a; Papageorgiou, 2013).

However, the conversion efficiency of light to energy by plants remains only 2–4% (Maxwell and Johnson, 2000a). Hence, the opportunity to improve the efficiency of photosynthesis has prompted a large number of researchers to enhance the existing photosynthetic efficiency in plant growth. Many strategies such as genetic improvement, gene manipulation and environmental modelling have been constructed to interfere and enhance the photosynthetic efficiency in crops. This genetic engineering modifications through biotechnology has proved effective in several plant species specifically in expanding trait development opportunities for high-quality trait determination. However, there are some drawbacks of genetic technology with respect to the cost of the computational tools for developing a variety of genetic approaches which are expensive and not accessible for all crops due to the requirements of a high-quality genome sequence (Nuccio et al., 2017). Thus, the inherent light interaction capabilities of quantum dots have emerged as a new promising approach towards producing high-performing crops to meet the needs of a growing global population (Murray et al., 2018).

Quantum dots (QD) are zero-dimensional nanostructures or also known as semi-conductor nanocrystals which are normally composed of atoms from group II-VI (e.g. ZnS, ZnSe and CdX (X = S, Se, Te) or group III-V (e.g. InP and InAs) elements in the periodic table. The effect of quantum confinement gives them unique optical and electronic properties that have potential applications in a variety of fields, such as optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, sensing and light/energy harvesting applications (Kairdolf et al., 2013; Govindjee. Energetics of, 2012; Nozik et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Maurino and Weber, 2013). Although semiconductor QD has brought many benefits to the world, this material has raised concerns over serious toxicity and environmental hazard to society (Chen et al., 2012). Therefore, carbon quantum dots (CQD) with an outstanding fluorescent property that consists of quasi-spherical carbon below 10 nm have drawn a great deal of attention for replacing semiconductor QD (Cao et al., 2012; Fernando et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2017; Yuan et al., 2016).

CQD has gradually become a rising star as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials due to their outstanding merits such as excellent water solubility, low cost, good photostability and environmental friendliness properties (e.g., good biocompatibility, inertness and low cytotoxicity) (Fu et al., 2016; Lim et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2014; Luo et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014). As a result, CQD has inspired extensive studies in a wide range of potential applications. However, there are still very few dedicated works focusing on the applications of CQD based stimulated electron transfer processes in agriculture.

Previously, researchers have incorporated CQD into isolated chloroplasts and observed that the electron transport rates were increased along with the enhancement of photosynthetic activity (Chandra et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2019; Chakravarty et al., 2015). CQD are thought to act as artificial antennae that enable chloroplasts to capture light wavelengths in different ranges, which included ultraviolet (UV), green and near-infrared. These studies demonstrated that CQD has tremendous potential advantages in agriculture and the significance of CQD for crop growth. The further elaboration on the properties of CQD for contributing to shaping the development of plant growth is summarized in Table 1 as follows.

Generally, there are various techniques involved in synthesizing CQD which covers arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, microwave synthesis, hydrothermal treatment, thermal decomposition, and plasma treatment (Li et al., 2011; Wang and Hu, 2014). Herein, we introduce a simple and facile microwave-assisted synthesis of CQD derived from EFB biochar (which is a by-product from the palm oil industry), a natural and renewable resource that is easily obtained in Malaysia. Microwave heating method can ultimately shorten the preparation time and provide simultaneous heating which leads to the discrete CQD with uniform size distribution. Plants can be divided into C3 and C4 plants depending on their different photosynthetic pathways. Usually, C3 plants exhibit a lower photosynthetic efficiency than most C4 plants. This report is the first to compare the ability of CQD in improving photosynthetic efficiency in C3 and C4 crops due to their inherent light interaction capabilities. CQD were applied on rice and corn which were used as representative models for C3 and C4 plants systems respectively. The growth, morphology, assimilation rate, stomatal conductance and the electron transfer behavior of the plants were further investigated to explore the influence of the CQD towards them. We further show that CQD introduced into rice and corn via foliar spraying application for the first time. This technique is simple, inexpensive, fast, and is applicable to varies crop species. The toxicity of CQD is an important aspect to be considered as it used for agriculture application, since this can result in environmental exposure. The toxicity of CQD to mouse embryonic fibroblast cell lines and zebrafish embryo were assessed, with zebrafish models mimic human disease both phenotypically and genetically.

Biochar samples used in this study were received from Pakar Go Green Sdn. Bhd made from EFB via a controlled pyrolysis process. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, C3H8O, 98%) was received from Merck. Deionized water was used throughout this study and all chemicals were used as received without further purification.

CQD was synthesized from biochar using a simple acid-free microwave-assisted process (Fig. 1 ). 1 g of biochar was dissolved in 100 mL of water and co-solvent (IPA) in a 3:1 ratio. Next, the mixture was transferred into a glass vessel and placed into a microwave. Heating was then set at a low power level setting (ranging from 100 to 400 W) for (1–9 min). After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant black CQD solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered using filter paper 601 (Filtres Fioroni, 125 mm diameter) for removing any remaining larger particles and impurities. CQD was further dialyzed using a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3.5 kDa) against deionized water for three days. Based on the final weight of larger particles, the concentration of the supernatant was estimated to be around 3000 ppm. The yield of CQD was calculated from the excess biochar subtracted from the initial weight of 1 g (refer S1). The effect of microwave heating time on CQD was investigated. The CQD solution was sent for further characterization and subsequently applied to C3 and C4 plants using simple foliar spraying technique.

High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2010) was used to study the morphology and particle size of CQD operating at a higher accelerating voltage up to 200 kV. The sample preparation for HRTEM analysis was initiated with a small amount of carbon dots suspension dispersed in IPA solution and sonicated for 5 min. Then, a drop of the suspension solution was placed on Cu grids covered with amorphous carbon film. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis (PerkinElmer LS 55) was applied to carry out electron transfer study of plants with CQD using Ar+ laser with a wavelength of 325 nm. UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800) was used to measure the absorbance of CQD. The elemental composition was determined using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) with a PHI Quantera II with Spherical Capacitor Analyzer (SCA) and a monochromatic Al Ka (1486.6 eV) source. The binding energies for the charge shift were corrected using the reference C 1s line of spurious carbon located at a binding energy of 284.8 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and calculation of fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield of CQD with different heating time was determined used Microplate Reader (Infinite 200 PRO, Tecan). The quantum yield (ϕ) of carbon quantum dots (CQD) was determined by comparing the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensities and the absorbance peak values of the CQD with quinine sulfate (QS) as a reference material. QS was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 and the CQD was dissolved in deionized water. The quantum yield of QS reported as 54% was selected as the standard reference due to the similar excitation and emission wavelength with the prepared CQD. The prepared CQD and QS were diluted to five different concentrations for absorbance measurement. Absorption spectra of all samples under 0.1 at 300 nm is determined to minimize re-absorption effects. In addition, their luminescent spectra with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm were also measured. The quantum yield was calculated using the following Equation (1) (Williams et al., 1983; Bera et al., 2010; Sadrolhosseini et al., 2019):
(1) Q = Qst(I/Ist) (n/nst)
where Q, n, I are the quantum yield, refractive index of solvent and integrated intensity of fluorescence spectrum, respectively. The subscript st refers to the standard sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthetic analysis of C3 and C4 plants
ce:section.ce:para:
Rice seeds variety IR64 and corn seeds variety ‘F1 Super Sweet’ variety were hydroponically grown using a standard AB nutrient solution under the condition of Light-Emitting Diode (LEDS) light. Rice and corn seed were germinated by soaking the seeds in the water and left in dark condition for two days. After two days, the germinated seeds were transferred to the hydroponics system. The temperature of the system was maintained at 30 °C and humidity maintained at 46% throughout the experiment in order to keep the plant grow at the same optimum condition. The Tween-20 medium was used to cultivate the plants. The effect of CQD on the photosynthesis of C3 (paddy) and C4 (corn) plants was investigated. Firstly, a certain amount of CQD (150 ppm) was mixed with water and surfactants (Tween-20). Before this, IPA was removed from CQD solution using gentle heating. Then, the mixed solution was then sprayed onto the leaves of both C3 and C4 plants respectively twice a month until the end of heading period. For the untreated rice and control plants, pure water as a control group and mixed with Tween-20 were sprayed onto the leaves of both control C3 and C4 plants respectively. The height of corn and rice crop, ear weight of corn and average weight per 1000 grains of rice were measured, and the data were recorded. Thereafter, the treated and untreated portion of the leaf blade was examined by measuring the photosynthetic CO2 assimilation rate and stomatal conductance (μmol CO2 m−2 s−1) using a portable photosynthesis system (LI-COR, Li-6400XT). In this experiment, the measurements were made 1 h after the light came on in the growth chamber at 9 a.m. Measurements were carried out at a temperature of 25 °C. The conditions used during the measurement were 10% blue light for the light at Photosynthetic active radiation (PAR) go section, the relative humidity turned to >50%, the infrared red gas analyzers (IRGAs) was set at zero and the “O” ring on the top of the CO2 tank was changed before use. The ability of water use in the leaf was measured by intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE) and the formula for calculating the iWUE is shown in Equation (2) (Loader et al., 2011):
(2)
iWUE =
CO 2
Assimilation rate
Stomatal conductance

The height of corn and rice crop, ear weight of corn and average weight per 1000 grains of rice were analysed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). The experiments were repeated for 4 times. The data were entered into Microsoft excel and all results were presented as mean ± standard error (SE).

The chloroplasts were extracted from 8 weeks corn and rice leaves during the vegetative stage using the Sigma Chloroplast Isolation Kit (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis MO, USA). Freshly harvested leaves of rice and corn were used throughout the experiment and all experiment steps were performed at 2 to 4 °C. Before isolating the chloroplast, the plants of rice and corn were kept in the dark one day before for optimal yield of intact chloroplast and avoiding high levels of starch accumulation. All the chloroplast suspensions were kept in the dark or on ice until further use. The detailed experiment steps for isolating chloroplasts was shown in S2. A schematic diagram on the isolation of chloroplast is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Chlorophyll fluorescence using PL spectroscopy was conducted to elucidate the electron transfer mechanism between CQD and chloroplasts. Initially, rice and corn seeds were selected with similar sizes. Then, the seeds were washed with deionized water three times to remove any residue exists. And then the seeds were transferred to a seedling tray for germination. CQD solution (150 ppm) was sprayed onto the leaves of rice and corn plants respectively and kept in the dark one day before the isolation of chloroplasts. In the control experiments, rice and corn plants without any application of CQD were set as control samples. The isolation of chloroplasts for both the control group and CQD-treated groups were prepared using the same procedures as mentioned in Section 2.7. Intact chloroplasts were brought for PL analysis on the same day. The chlorophyll fluorescence of rice and corn were carried out under 390 nm excitation wavelength.

MTT assay (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) method was chosen to measure the cytotoxicity of CQD solution. Cell lines used in this study were 3T3 cells (Mouse embryonic fibroblast cell lines) bought from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) (Manassas, VA, USA) and grown in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) media. A concentration of 2 × 103 cells/ml was prepared for the cell culture and was plated (100 μL/well) into 96-well plates. Each well plate was added with the diluted ranges of CQD solution with concentrations of 1000, 500, 200, 100, 50, 20 and 1 ppm followed by 72 h incubation. Then MTT solution was added to the cells and incubation was continued for another 3 h. Thereafter, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used for solubilization of the purple formazan crystals and ELISA reader was used to measuring the Optical Density (OD) of the extract at a wavelength of 570 nm.

The relative cell viability (%) can be defined as a percentage in accordance with the untreated control cells as presented by Equation (3). If the absorbance value of the sample is greater than the absorbance control, it is known as cell proliferation while inhibition of proliferation occurs when the absorbance value of the sample is lower than the absorbance control.
(3)
Cell viability =
Absorbance sample
( mean )
Absorbance control
( mean )
× 100 %

The zebrafish embryotoxicity test is another popular method to determine cytotoxicity. The Danio Assay Kit used was purchased from Danio Assay Laboratories Sdn Bhd, UPM. The approval of zebrafish use was given by University Putra Malaysia Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee. Zebrafish embryos were collected immediately after fertilization. Normal embryos at the blastocyst stage were randomly selected and introduced individually into 96-well plates filled with 150 μL/well 0.2% embryos media solution (EMS). The embryos were incubated for 24 h which is known as 24 h post-fertilization (hpf-24 h). The developmental toxicity effects of CQD on the subsequent embryonic and larvae development against control were investigated by exposing fertilized embryos to a range of concentrations (0, 32.5, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000 mg/L). In our study, embryos/larvae from the five early embryonic development stages (24 hpe, 48 hpe, 72 hpe, 96 hpe and 120 hpe) were analysed by observation using optical microscopy (Nikon SMZ 25, Japan). The morphological development of zebrafish at each stage was photographed and video recorded with a digital microscope imaging camera (DinoCapture 2.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A facile and economical one-step CQD synthesis based on microwave treatment of empty fruit bunch biochar is presented. The influence of microwave heating time on the photophysical properties (absorption and PL emission spectra) of CQD has been evaluated. Microwaves are electromagnetic radiation which can be absorbed by polar molecules to be converted into heat via dielectric heating. This phenomenon is caused by dielectric polarization and conduction loss where polar molecules, which have an electrical dipole moment undergo molecular rotation when irradiated with microwaves. In the process of microwave synthesis, a short reaction time is the goal that is often desirable.

The influence of the microwave reaction time on the UV, photoluminescence (PL) and the colour of CQDs were investigated as illustrated in Fig. 3 . Under microwave irradiation, CQDs with blue emission wavelength were produced by varying the reaction time. The UV–vis spectrum of the CQD aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 3 (a). An absorption peak at 226 nm is seen which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of graphitic sp (Porcar-Castell et al., 2014) domains. Another broad peak at 320 nm was also observed, which could be attributed to the n- π* transition of CO bond. The broadened peak may be assigned to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states resulting in strong emission (Zhu et al., 2015). Considering CQD as luminescent carbon-based nanoparticles, thus, the solution was able to emit radiation during exposure to the short-wavelength UV lamp. The absorption wavelength for CQD obtained at different reaction heating time at low power setting with fixed biochar solution (1 g: 100 mL) was measured as shown in Fig. 3a. The intensities of peak 226 nm increase with the heating time due to increasing of CQD conversion yield as this could be supported by the conversion yield as represented in Table 2 . The reaction is ultrafast, and it will take 1 min to extract CQD. CQD can be extracted from EFB biochar using hydrothermal method as reported by Jamaludin et al. (2020), however, longer heating time was required if using hydrothermal method.

In Table 2, quantum yield of CQD at different heating time is demonstrated and the highest achieved quantum yield of this study was compared with other works. The slope of fluorescence integral intensity to the absorbance was used to calculate the quantum yield using Equation (1) and the refractive index was measured using surface plasmon resonance. The refractive index of CQD and quinine sulfate were 1.371 and 1.4023, respectively. Table 2 shows that the quantum yield increased as the heating time increased from 1 to 7 min and these results are consistent with the CQD conversion yield results. The relatively high quantum yield of CQD obtained in this study implied that the CQD are well surface passivated by carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the edges of the quantum dots. The produced CQD were matched with various natural precursors with size and quantum yield are summarized in Table 3 . The prepared CQD exhibited a larger quantum yield compared to other natural precursors, revealed that the EFB biochar used in this study enhanced the quantum yields to a certain extent.

A longer microwave heating time results in more energy being received by the solvent which will increase its temperature. Microwave absorption induces a very rapid rising of the temperature of reagents, solvents, and products. When microwaves are applied to chemical processes, the overheating of the solvent is a frequent phenomenon that can be observed. The direct absorption of microwave radiation occurs in the bulk of the solvent and the transfer of energy is no longer mediated by the surface of the vessel. The reaction rate can be accelerated by 2 times relative for every 10 °C rise in temperature, thus this explains how the reactions in a solution can be speeded up in the presence of microwaves radiation (Fini and Breccia, 1999a). Sari et al. (2018) reported that an increase in microwave irradiation time can result in a linear increase of extracted pectin yield from watermelon rinds.

A detailed PL investigation was carried out with different excitation wavelengths to further explore the optical properties of the water-soluble CQD. The basic photoluminescence mechanism of CQD can be briefly explained as follows. Initially, the absorption of the radiation will cause the excitation electrons in CQD from an orbital in the high occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to a lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Then, the de-excitation of the emission process occurs, and the electrons will return to the ground state. Fig. 3(b) shows the PL emission spectra of CQD prepared at different reaction times under excitation at 325 nm. As the reaction heating time increases, PL emission intensity also increases and the prepared CQD at 9 min gave the highest luminescence with a bright blue colour. It can be observed that the PL emission intensity is directly proportional with the reaction heating time up to 7 min, the heating time of 9 min shows slightly lower in terms of conversion yield as compared to 7 min. The volume of the solution at 9 min decreased due to overheating occurred resulting in the decrement of conversion yield.

The corresponding PL spectra of CQD at 7 min with different excitation wavelengths at 325–450 nm are shown in Fig. 3 (c). It was found that as the excitation wavelength is progressively increased from 325 to 450 nm, the PL peak shifts to longer emission wavelengths and its intensity decreases rapidly. This phenomenon demonstrates the excitation-dependent emission property of CQD that agrees well with other reports (Zhu et al., 2015; van Dam et al., 2017; Roy et al., 2015). This wavelength-dependent behavior may result from the nanoparticle's size and surface chemistry or energy traps created by surface groups (Zhang and Yu, 2016). From a previous report, a plausible reason for the wavelength-dependent PL properties of CQD was due to homogeneous size distribution of nanoparticles as observed in the HRTEM image (Fini and Breccia, 1999b). Moreover, CQD with higher oxidation degrees may possess more surface states resulting in PL emissions with varied energies at different excitations. The oxide defects were generated by the oxidation facilitated on the surface of CQD, introducing complex surface states to trap the electrons under excitations. Then, the PL emission with corresponding energy was introduced by radiative recombination of the trapped electron or excitons. The corresponding PL emissions of CQD covered the UV/Vis wavelength range, from violet (~430 nm) to yellow-green (~550 nm). A photoluminescence emission peak at 430 nm (highest emission intensity) was obtained upon excitation of an aqueous solution of CQD at 325 nm.

The luminescence properties of the as-prepared CQD solutions were investigated by irradiation with 365 nm UV light. The UV–vis spectra of an aqueous solution of CQD showed no optical absorption beyond 400 nm, thus, the CQD solutions were transparent yet greyish in daylight for all the samples and exhibited a bright blue luminescence when excited with UV light. The colour of the CQD solution could be easily seen with the naked eye and taken with a digital phone camera as shown in Fig. 3(d). Throughout the ongoing reaction, the initially light blue liquid sample turned into a brighter deep blue as the reaction progressed. During microwave heating, the bulk solution temperature increased steadily which would lead to a rapid increase of the internal pressure of the system. At the longer heating time, the vapour pressure of water was likely to be high, as indicated by the loss of bulk solution via evaporation. This could be observed in Fig. 3(d) whereby the volume of the solution began to decrease at heating times higher than 5 min. In order to avoid any overheating in the experimental set-up, the heating time of 5 min was selected to produce CQD for the agricultural study that will be discussed further in the section below.

To determine the morphology and structure of the CQD, the as-prepared sample was characterized by HRTEM. From Fig. 4 (a) and (b), the micrograph images show that the as-prepared CQD sample were well-dispersed within the solution, with virtually minimal aggregation. The CQD appear circular with a narrow size distribution (judging from 50 random particles). An average diameter of 2.6 ± 0.6 nm was obtained using Image J software as displayed in the size distribution histogram of Fig. 4 (c). Furthermore, in Fig. 4 (d), the image clearly shows highly crystalline lattice fringes of CQD have a lattice spacing measuring 0.21 nm. This particular spacing corresponds to the in-plane lattice spacing of (100) diffraction facets of sp (Porcar-Castell et al., 2014) graphitic carbon (d100= 0.213 nm) (Du et al., 2015).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed to confirm the formation and elemental composition of CQD, specifically for the examination of functional groups on the surface of the CQD. The XPS survey scan of CQD is shown in Fig. 5 (a). Distinct graphitic C1s and O1s signals were observed at 284 and 532 eV; respectively, indicating that CQD mainly consists of C and O elements. Meanwhile, potassium elements with K 2s, 2p and K 3s, 3p peaks were observed in the spectrum which is attributed to the ash contents in biochar (K: 20.29 wt%; Na: 8.15 wt%; Si: 6.19 wt%). As depicted in Fig. 5 (b), the deconvolution of high-resolution C1s spectrum showed four representative peaks at 284.7, 285.8, 287.9, and 289.0 eV, which corresponds to C–C, C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively. The primary and dominant peak at 284.7 eV can be designated to the formed graphitic carbon. This peak indicates that the prepared CQD possess sp (Porcar-Castell et al., 2014) graphitic structure. Meanwhile, the high resolution O1s spectrum in Fig. 5 (c) can be deconvoluted into several peaks at 530.5, 531.8, and 534.1 eV, which can be attributed to C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively. Noticeably, the presence of oxygen functional groups revealed that the surface of CQD is functionalized by multiple oxygenated groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups) and leads to the hydrophilic nature of CQD (Zhang et al., 2012). The CQD is stable and well-dispersed in water due to the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon quantum dots on the physiological traits of rice and corn
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthetic CO2 assimilation rate, stomatal conductance and water use efficiency were used as parameters for expressing the photosynthetic rate and physiological traits of rice and corn. The concentration of CQD selected in the physiological experiment was 150 mg/L. This concentration was chosen because preliminary screening studies on the effects of four different concentrations of CQD (50, 100, 150 and 200 ppm) showed that 150 mg/L was the lowest concentration that was able to yield visible physiological effects during the vegetative growth stage. Photosynthesis of plants consist a two-step process which is known as light-dependent and light-independent reactions. The detailed description on photosynthesis process was described in S3.

CQD as a good electron donor and acceptor, have played an important role in the rate of photosynthesis and have been employed in the light energy conversion applications (Wientjes et al., 2017). Up to now, the potential impacts that CQD have on various crop plant growth have attracted more and more attention from researchers worldwide (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015; Chen et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2017b; Wang et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018). An overview of the impacts of CQD on the growth of various plants in agriculture is summarized in Table 4 .

CQD has a significant effect on the growth and development of plant height and weight as shown in Fig. 6 . Compared to the control, the height of rice treated with CQD increased by about 20.9% significantly, however, CQD had little effect on the height of the CQD-corn (increased by 4.9% only). The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQD provide abundant binding sites for water molecules, which enter the plant along with the CQD. The adequate moisture in plant leaves can promote the growth of plant and accelerate the height of plant (Li et al., 2020a). As shown in Fig. 6 (b) and (c), the ear weight of corn and average weight per 1000 grains are significantly increased after CQD treatment compared with the controls, indicating the corn and rice yield is elevated by CQD.

CO2 assimilation is a process which involves the conversion of CO2 into simple organic compounds (carbohydrates) in the absence of sunlight. As shown in Fig. 7 , the photosynthetic CO2 assimilation rate of rice and corn responded differently to the presence of CQD. Rice is a C3 plants while corn is a C4 plants which having different photosynthesis pathways. The assimilation rate (Fig. 7a) values of rice showed that CQD significantly improved these parameters by 56% more than the control. Rice utilizes the C3 pathway to fix carbon through the Calvin cycle. ATP and NADPH formed during the light reactions are utilized for CO2 to be reduced to triose phosphate in the reaction of C3 cycle (Singh et al., 2014; Raines, 2011). In C3 cycle, the first stable compound form is phosphoglycerate (3-PGA) and consist of three carbon atoms. For each molecule of CO2 that is fixed, 3 molecules of ATP and 2 molecules of NADPH from the light reactions are needed. The CO2 assimilation in rice was enhanced by the CQD with a high rate of photosynthetic carbon reaction, suggesting that the treated rice plants are able to perform more photochemistry with the light that they absorb.

On the other hand, the corn plant treated with CQD show a maximum increase of only 16% in carbon assimilation rate as compared to the control. Corn being a C4 plant, having higher rates of photosynthesis and growth compared to C3 crop as C4 pathway works by reducing photorespiration and increasing in absolute rates of CO2 fixation. The first stable product of carbon fixation in C4 pathway is 4-carbon oxaloacetate instead of 3 carbon compounds as in C3 plants. C4 pathway is proven to be more efficient than the C3 pathway in the sense of carbon fixation plants as the light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle are physically separated for fixing CO2. The light-dependent reactions take place in mesophyll cells while the Calvin cycle occurs in bundle-sheath cells. The C4 pathway work by ensuring that Rubisco (carbon-fixing enzyme of the Calvin cycle) always encounters high concentrations of CO2 and reduce the oxygenase activity of rubisco (Lara and Andreo, 2011; Silva et al., 2020). As a result, we can observe from Fig. 7 (b) that the control-corn have higher carbon assimilation rate than the control-rice. This is the plausible reason why CQD had a greater effect on the treated rice than the corn as the untreated corn plant itself already has an established higher photosynthetic rate compared to rice. Increases in CO2 assimilation resulted in higher electron transport rates as CQD promotes light assimilation and boosts the number of excitons and electron transfer efficiencies in photosystem (PS) I and II (Iermak et al., 2016; Zienkiewicz et al., 2015). Therefore, it is inferred that CQD help to transfer electron during the photosynthesis process and induces an increase in CO2 assimilation.

The impacts of CQD on electron transfer has been previously reported by Chandra et al., but so far none reported the CQD effects on stomatal conductance of C3 and C4 crops. Stomatal conductance is a measure of the degree of stomatal opening and can be used to regulate photosynthesis and transpiration process of plants. Stomatal regulation of gas exchange by CQD is of great importance, which become the focus of this study. Plants need stomata on their leaves to take in CO2 and regulates gas exchange for CO2 and water and thereby increase their CO2 uptake for enhancement of photosynthesis (Raines, 2010; Xu et al., 2016). Fig. 7c shows that CQD promotes the stomatal conductance in rice with the improvement of 56% as compared to the control rice. Since higher stomatal conductance may enhance CO2 diffusion into chloroplasts and to the increased capacity of the mesophyll to carry out CO2 assimilation, this indicated that CQD enhanced gas exchanged capacity and the potential productivity of the plants. Also, improvement in stomatal conductance allowed the plants to optimize stomatal movement, leading to an enhancement in water status.

On the contrary, CQD effects on stomatal conductance of corn was relatively statistically small with a maximum increase of only 18% as compared to the control (Fig. 7d). It was observed that the control corn had higher stomatal conductance than control rice, which may be due to decreased photorespiration in C4 crops and a higher capability for CO2 assimilation. This study provides us with a phylogenetically constrained comparison of CQD on stomatal conductance in plants with two different photosynthetic pathways, which is useful in understanding the underlying mechanism of the regulation of stomatal behavior.

Water is essential for plant growth as it provides the medium within which most cellular functions take place. Intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE) was defined as the ratio of net CO2 assimilation to stomatal conductance and it implies the inherent ability of the plant to assimilate CO2 (Gilbert et al., 2011). Being a ratio, higher iWUE could be obtained by reducing stomatal conductance per amount of CO2 assimilated or by enhancing the assimilate rate at a given stomatal conductance. Stomata are the “gatekeepers” responsible for improving water use efficiency and, at the same time minimize water loss through transpiration (Lawson and Blatt, 2014). Improving iWUE of plants is considered advantageous in very dry climates, hence, water-conserving traits as imposed by higher iWUE could be beneficial, provided growth and photosynthesis are not negatively affected. In this study, the iWUE in both control and treated rice did not differ significantly as shown in Fig. 7e. It is important to note that CQD did not negatively impact the iWUE for rice despite the 56% improvement in photosynthesis. This indicated that treated rice maintains a higher photosynthetic rate as well as higher values of stomatal conductance, which is a very significant finding with the slightly differ in iWUE. From the literature, plants such as Oryza sativa (rice), Avena sativa (oat) and Triticum aestivum (wheat) showed high photosynthetic rates but with low iWUE which are the signal for traditional breeding and selection practices for high yield at the expense of water loss (McAusland et al., 2016). Usually when there is intervention to increase photosynthesis, the iWUE decreases, therefore, this would be another advantage of using CQD for the future of agriculture practices. As for corn, iWUE decreases because CQD does not significantly affect CO2 assimilation rate (Fig. 7f). Adding CQD is not advantageous for C4 plants because photosynthesis is not enhanced yet stomata is forced to open unnecessarily leading to a drop in iWUE. The higher value of iWUE in control corn could be attributed to the CO2 concentration mechanism (CCM) in C4 photosynthesis which causes a reduction in transpiration rate by increasing the CO2 concentration in the bundle sheath cells, allowing high assimilation at low rates of transpiration (Hamim, 2005).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo induced electron transfer study in plants using carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Around 80% of the captured light is used for photosynthesis process under ideal condition while the remaining light is dissipated as heat or re-emitted as chlorophyll fluorescence (Schreiber et al., 1995). Hence, the amount of chlorophyll fluorescence emission could be a direct indicator of the photosynthetic activity of a plant (Schweiger et al., 1996; Van Kooten and Snel, 1990). The chlorophyll fluorescence properties were the most concerning issues for CQD in view of investigating the photoluminescence and electron transfer mechanism between CQD and chloroplast. CQD was chosen to pair with chloroplast because they can absorb light in the same wavelength (UV/Vis) region where the wavelength of light is essential for normal plant growth. Therefore, a single wavelength can excite both the CQD and the chloroplasts, which leads to electrons being boosted from the CQD to chloroplasts and chloroplasts to the photosystems. Electrons are vital for photosynthesis where the light energy is absorbed by the pigments in the antenna. Then, the energy of their excited electrons is transferred to a chlorophyll molecule that serves as a reaction centre. The reaction centre is a single chlorophyll that shows the highest absorption at 430 nm and 660 nm (Chandra et al., 2014; Mishra, 2004; Li et al., 2018b).

Fig. 8 a displays the chlorophyll fluorescence spectral pattern of the individual CQD, chloroplast isolation buffer (CIB) and a mixture of CIB with the chloroplasts of rice (C3) and corn (C4) under 390 nm excitation. The fluorescence emission for both rice and corn are contributed by Photosystem I and II (PSI, PSII). From the fluorescence spectra shown, a total of two characteristic fluorescence bands were found in CIB as well as the mixture of CIB with the chloroplasts of rice and corn. All these peaks show a broad blue fluorescence around 440–450 nm (F450) as well as a red fluorescence peak near 690 nm (F690). F690 mostly originates from PSII, where PSII is active in the far-red to the near-infrared region between 650 and 780 nm. The F690 emission spectra of rice exhibited much lower fluorescence intensities when compared to the broad F450. A broad fluorescence emission in the red-light region was observed for both the chloroplast of corn plant as well as the complex of CQD with corn plant shown in Fig. 8b, suggesting that the intensity of the deep emission is flat above the threshold of the PL equipment. Furthermore, corn has an extra broader shoulder fluorescence peak from 700 to 750 nm which is attributed by both PSII and PSI where the PSI only emits in wavelengths >700 nm (Peterson et al., 2014).

As presented in Fig. 8b, for the chloroplast of rice and corn coupled with the mixture of CQD, respectively, both plants showed the fluorescence emission bands in the red-light region. It is noted that red-light emissions are easily obtained in the presence of chlorophyll and blue emissions are commonly observed in the CQD only. CQD exhibited high fluorescence property and can act as an electron donor after association with chloroplast of the plant. It is speculated that CQD has a resonance energy transfer with chloroplast as energy donors which improves the light energy utilization efficiency. Chloroplast will receive the excess electrons from CQD and then electrons are transported to PSII and PSI, resulting in an increase of the total electron transport chain during photosynthesis. The chlorophyll fluorescence peak of PSI which absorb at wavelengths above F700 was not observed in this study due to the low energy chlorophyll of PSI (Surendran et al., 2020; Peterson et al., 2014; Maxwell and Johnson, 2000b). The photochemical steps involving the energy and electron transfer from PSII to PSI happen rapidly. When the excitation energy reaches on PSII centre, the transfer of excitation energy (F680 → F680+) occurs on a time scale of a nanosecond to femtoseconds. The ultrafast events of electron transfer from the antenna to the reaction centre and for the CQD electron transfer process are supported by the reduction in emission peak of CQD at 463 nm for both rice and corn as compared with the original peak of CQD in Fig. 8a. This enhancement of acceptor emission phenomenon can be further explained by the CQD directly absorbed the emitted photons along with the energy transfer from CQD to the chloroplast, supported by the work done by Chandra et al., 2014) (Porcar-Castell et al., 2014) on a mung bean plant. This qualitative finding points towards an electrochemical interaction between CQD and chloroplast. Furthermore, it was noticed that the F450 fluorescence emission intensity for rice was much higher compared to corn plant. This means that corn plant is better protected against UV irradiation than rice plants where the UV-absorbing substance is accumulated in their epidermis cells to prevent the exciting UV irradiation from penetrating through the epidermis into the mesophyll layer of the plant (Caffarri et al., 2014; Dalal and Tripathy, 2018). CQD plays an essential role to augment the photosynthesis by light conversion with highly tunable fluorescence emission under UV radiation. In our study, the excitation-dependent emission behavior of CQD is an extraordinary photophysical characteristics that leads to tunable photoluminescence nanomaterials that originates from the surface states of CQD. It was observed that the presence of surface functional groups (-COOH and –CO) at the edge of CQD will improve the PL quantum efficiency. Considering the crucial role of the light intensity in photosynthesis, the quantum yield is an utmost important factor of CQD that affects the plant photosynthesis. From the previous literature, the too low (15.13%) or too high (90.59%) quantum yield will cause the insignificant influence even inhibition of CQD on the photosynthesis activities of chloroplast (Li et al., 2020b). Low quantum yield of CQD will possibly interferes with the light absorption of the chloroplast while high quantum yield of CQD will cause photoinhibition to the chloroplasts. The finding indicated the moderate quantum yield of CQD at 46.42% will promote the plant growth as a light converter for the photosynthesis. As a result, CQD obtained in our study with quantum yield of 53.7% suggesting having significant increase to plant photosynthesis.

CQD improve photosynthetic light-harvesting and induce electron transfer can be supported by gas exchange results. The maximum rate of photosynthetic electron transport (Jmax) is one of the gas exchange characteristics derived from the light response curve and carbon dioxide response curve (A/Ci). Jmax is closely related to the leaves’ ability to transport electrons to produce ATP and NADPH which are the major drivers for the photosynthetic carbon cycle. Fig. 8c shows the Jmax results of control and CQD-treated rice and corn plants. In our study, the increased Jmax was accompanied by significantly increased whole chain electron transport in both CQD treated rice (+57.3%) and corn (+65.5%) leaves compared with that of the control. It is well known that an increased electron transport capacity leads to an increase in Ribulose 1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP) regeneration capacity and results in increased consumption of equivalent energy in the Calvin cycle (Simkin et al., 2019). As discussed previously, it was found that plants treated with CQD cause a drastic increase in CO2 assimilation rate. An increase in the capacity of CO2 assimilation in the Calvin cycle would increase the demand for ATP and NADPH, resulting in increased electron transport to match the higher demand for ATP and NADPH. The increase in electron transport was attributed to both the increase in the number of open PSII reaction centres and the efficiency of energy capture by these open centres. This implies that an increase in the maximum rate of electron transport stimulates the photosynthesis process, whereby CQD can act as an alternative electron sink that promotes electron transport from CQD to the chloroplast in PSII and PSI system. Enhancing electron transport and Calvin cycle activity can enhance the overall photosynthetic capacity of leaves. The results shed some light on the mechanisms of CQD responses for improving photosynthetic electron transport that drives improvements in plant growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity testing (MTT and embryos assay methods)
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity is the killing ability of synthesized chemicals, naturally occurring toxins or immune-mediator cells. The MTT assay method was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared CQD before agricultural application as shown in Fig. 9 . The graph was plotted with the percentage of cell viability against their respective concentrations (1000, 500, 200, 100, 50, 20 and 1 ppm) after the determination of the percentage of cell viability. In the current study, the cytotoxicity of CQD was dose-dependent, the lowest concentrations of CQD were not significantly different from the control group. The CQD exhibit very low cytotoxicity, the viability of over 80% at a concentration of 200 ppm after 72 h incubation while the maximum cell deaths (42%) show at a concentration of 1000 ppm. The viability of cells was over 50% even when the concentration of CQD was elevated to 800 ppm. It is noteworthy that the obtained CQD exhibit good biocompatibility and low toxicity toward 3T3 cells (Mouse embryonic fibroblast cell lines) even at high doses (800 mg/L) after long incubation times (72 h); thus, they are considered safe and suitable for use in agricultural applications. The result also demonstrates the value of IC50 (50% inhibition) of CQD on 3T3 cells after 72hr exposure occurs at 900 ppm.

Biocompatibility and the in-vivo biological toxicity of the as-synthesized CQD can be further validated by using zebrafish embryos and larvae. Zebrafish was chosen as a model for the toxicological experiments due to their rapid embryonic development and low cost and ease of handling. 120 h exposure of newly fertilized eggs in a static or semi-static system was used in the zebrafish embryotoxicity test as the embryonic development of zebrafish is rapidly completed within 120 h. As toxicological endpoints, coagulation/dead of embryos, no heartbeat, somite formation as well as non-detachment of the tail from the yolk are recorded after 24–120 h exposure to CQD. The zebrafish larvae grow normally after soaking in (1000, 500, 250, 125, 62.5 and 31.25 mg/L) CQD solution. As shown in Fig. 10 a, there were no obvious developmental defects were observed for the concentration group treatments (31.25–1000 mg/L) as compared with the control groups. No significant change among all concentration group treatments was detected before 120 h post-exposure (hpe) as the CQD did not affect the development of larva within this period time. From work of literature, the zebrafish embryotoxicity was normally recorded, and the embryo was examined up to 120 hpe only (Huang et al., 2010; Li et al., 2018c). CQD showed higher tolerance concentration by zebrafish embryos and larvae as compared with other reported quantum dots (Wang et al., 2015). The developmental stages of zebrafish embryos at 0–120 hpe for 2000 ppm CQD solution was illustrated in Fig. 10b. It can be observed that the embryos had a hatching period started from 48 hpe. In 2000 mg/L concentration of CQD, the zebrafish larvae showed malformation indicated by the death of larvae, no heartbeat and tail/body curvature. The result confirmed the good biocompatibility of our CQD to be applied for the agricultural application.

In conclusion, CQD have been successfully prepared from empty fruit bunch (EFB) biochar using a simple and facile microwave method. In this work, light-harvesting CQD was explored as a photosynthesis enhancer for improving the photosynthetic rate and stomatal conductance in relation to carbon assimilation and intrinsic water use efficiency of C3 and C4 plants. The work presented here illustrates that the photosynthetic rate and stomatal conductance in rice and corn could be gained by up to 56% and 18% with the presence of CQD. To our knowledge, this is the first report about the comparative study on the CO2 assimilation rate, stomatal conductance and intrinsic water use efficiency of CQD in rice and corn plants. In-vivo biological toxicity of CQD in the zebrafish embryo does not show cytotoxicity up to 1000 mg/mL. The biocompatible use of CQD in plant growth could play an important role in the area of agriculture for enhanced production and contribute to the development of research at the interface between nanotechnology and plant physiology.

T.L.T. wrote the manuscript and performed the experiment with support from S.A.R. and M.N.Y. A.S.K.Z. and N.A.Z carried out the agricultural experiment, while A.S.K.Z. and M.B.J. conceived of the presented idea. M.N.Y. analysed and interpreted the data; S. A. R. supervised the project. All authors reviewed the final manuscript.
